questions.question: Pneumothorax refers to:

options.B: Air in the pleural cavity causing lung collapse

questions.question: The partial pressure of O2 in alveoli is approximately:
Surfactant in the alveoli is produced by:
The diagram shows the oxygen-haemoglobin dissociation curve. What happens to the curve when CO2 increases (Bohr effect)?
questions.diagram_description: Graph showing O2-Hb dissociation curve (% saturation of Hb vs pO2). Normal sigmoid curve shown. Second curve shifted to the right labeled 'increased CO2/decreased pH (Bohr effect)'. Third curve shifted to left labeled 'decreased CO2/increased pH'. Points at pO2=40 and pO2=100 mmHg marked

options.B: Curve shifts to right (lower affinity, more O2 released)

questions.question: Emphysema leads to reduced gas exchange because:

options.B: Alveolar walls break down reducing surface area

questions.question: The chloride shift (Hamburger phenomenon) involves:

options.B: Cl- entering RBCs as HCO3- exits

questions.question: The diagram shows gas exchange at the alveolar surface. Which gas moves from alveolus to blood?
questions.diagram_description: Diagram of alveolus and adjacent pulmonary capillary showing: alveolar air (pO2=104mmHg, pCO2=40mmHg), deoxygenated blood entering (pO2=40mmHg, pCO2=45mmHg), gas exchange across thin alveolar-capillary membrane (two cell layers + basement membrane = 0.5 micron), oxygenated blood leaving (pO2=95mmHg, pCO2=40mmHg). Arrows showing O2 and CO2 movements

options.A: O2 moves from blood to alveolus
options.B: CO2 moves from alveolus to blood
options.C: O2 moves from alveolus to blood
options.D: Both gases move from alveolus to blood

questions.question: Carbaminohaemoglobin is formed when:

options.B: CO2 binds to globin part of Hb

questions.question: The functional residual capacity (FRC) is:
During forced expiration, which muscles contract?

options.C: Internal intercostal muscles and abdominal muscles

questions.question: The enzyme carbonic anhydrase is found in:
The diagram shows lung volumes and capacities. What is the total lung capacity (TLC) approximately?
questions.diagram_description: Spirometer tracing showing lung volumes: RV=1200mL, ERV=1100mL, TV=500mL, IRV=3000mL. Capacities derived: FRC=RV+ERV=2300mL, VC=IRV+TV+ERV=4600mL, TLC=VC+RV=5800mL. Graph shows normal breathing then maximal inspiration and expiration with all volumes labeled

questions.question: Which type of alveolar cell performs phagocytosis?
The Hering-Breuer reflex prevents:
Hypercapnia means:
During exercise, the O2-Hb dissociation curve shifts to the right because:

options.B: Temperature, CO2 and H+ increase in working muscles

questions.question: Silicosis is caused by:
The pleural fluid serves to:

options.B: Reduce friction between lung and chest wall

questions.question: The diagram shows the mechanism of CO2 transport. In which form is most CO2 transported?
questions.diagram_description: Diagram showing CO2 produced in tissue cells entering RBC. Three pathways: (1) ~7% dissolved in plasma; (2) ~23% as carbaminoHb binding to globin chains; (3) ~70% reacting with H2O via carbonic anhydrase to form H2CO3, which dissociates to H+ (buffered by Hb) and HCO3- (exits RBC via chloride shift). Percentages labeled on each pathway

questions.question: Dead space air is air that:

options.B: Fills conducting passages and does not participate in gas exchange

questions.question: In fetal circulation, the ductus arteriosus connects:

options.B: Pulmonary artery to aorta bypassing lungs

questions.question: CO has ~200 times higher affinity for haemoglobin than O2. This causes:

options.B: Formation of carboxyhaemoglobin reducing O2 transport

questions.question: The diagram shows a cross-section of the trachea. What prevents the trachea from collapsing?
questions.diagram_description: Cross-section of trachea showing: pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium lining, C-shaped cartilage rings (incomplete posteriorly), trachealis muscle (smooth muscle) completing the ring posteriorly, submucosa with goblet cells and seromucous glands, adventitia on outside. Labeled: cartilage C-rings, trachealis muscle, lumen, epithelium

questions.question: Which chemoreceptors are most sensitive to changes in blood CO2?
COPD (Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease) is mainly caused by:
Pulmonary surfactant reduces:
The diagram compares normal alveoli with emphysematous alveoli. What is the main change in emphysema?
questions.diagram_description: Side-by-side comparison: Normal lung showing small numerous alveoli with thin walls, extensive capillary network, large total surface area (70 m²). Emphysematous lung showing enlarged air spaces due to destruction of alveolar walls (breakdown of elastin), fewer but larger alveoli, reduced capillary bed, reduced total surface area, loss of elastic recoil

options.B: Loss of alveolar walls reducing surface area

questions.question: Alveolar ventilation is greater than minute ventilation because:

options.B: Dead space volume is subtracted from tidal volume
options.C: Blood flow is faster in alveolar capillaries

questions.question: Oxygen toxicity at high pO2 levels can cause:

options.B: Damage to lung tissue and seizures

questions.question: The diagram shows gas partial pressures at different points in the circulation. What is the pO2 of venous blood returning to lungs?
questions.diagram_description: Diagram of circulation showing pO2 and pCO2 values: atmospheric air (pO2=159, pCO2=0.3 mmHg), alveolar air (pO2=104, pCO2=40), arterial blood leaving lungs (pO2=95, pCO2=40), tissue capillaries where O2 is consumed and CO2 produced, venous blood returning to lungs (pO2=40, pCO2=45 mmHg)

questions.question: Neonatal respiratory distress syndrome is due to:

options.B: Deficiency of surfactant in premature infants
